content.text: Two of the most powerful structural patterns in object-oriented design —

— appear throughout every serious agent framework. Inheritance lets a child class absorb and extend the attributes and methods of a parent class, eliminating boilerplate. Polymorphism lets orchestration code call a single method on a collection of objects and receive type-appropriate behaviour — so a tool dispatcher never needs to know whether it is running a web-search tool, a code-execution sandbox, or a vector-memory lookup. Together they form the backbone of extensible, testable agent systems.

is a compile-time (or definition-time) relationship between two classes. The child class (subclass) is declared to extend the parent class (base class or superclass). The child automatically acquires every attribute and method defined on the parent. It may then
individual methods to change their behaviour while leaving the rest intact, or it may add entirely new methods and attributes. In Python the syntax is simply
specialised form of the parent — this is the defining condition for a healthy inheritance relationship.

(from Greek: "many forms") is the property that a single interface — a method name, a function signature — can operate on objects of different concrete types and produce appropriate results for each. In Python this falls out of duck typing: any object that exposes the expected method works, whether or not it inherits from a shared base class. When combined with inheritance, polymorphism is enforced at the type level: a
, or any future subclass, and calling
on it always reaches the correct implementation.
Python uses a mechanism called the

to determine which method implementation to invoke when a hierarchy involves multiple levels or even multiple inheritance. The MRO is computed using the C3 linearisation algorithm and is accessible at runtime via
traverses an unexpected path in a multi-inheritance scenario.

Production agentic systems are never deployed with a single LLM provider, a single tool, or a single memory backend. Requirements evolve: a prototype uses OpenAI's API, then the team adds Anthropic as a fallback, then an open-source model for on-premise deployments. Without a shared abstract interface, each addition requires editing the orchestration loop — the most complex, most tested part of the codebase.
Inheritance solves this by centralising the contract in a base class. Polymorphism ensures the orchestration loop never needs to change: it calls
regardless of which concrete client is injected at runtime. This is the
in practice — the system is open for extension (add a new provider subclass) and closed for modification (the orchestrator does not change).

Testability also improves dramatically. Because every tool, client, and memory store inherits from a shared interface, a unit test can substitute a lightweight fake implementation without patching modules or monkey-patching objects. The test injects the fake through the same constructor or factory the production code uses.
— shared logic lives once in the base class; subclasses override only the parts that genuinely differ. A
can be composed into any tool without duplicating retry and logging logic in each concrete class.
— any code typed against the base class accepts any subclass, making provider and backend swaps transparent to callers. Unit tests inject fakes through the same path production code uses.
— new tool types, new model providers, and new memory backends can be added by writing a single new subclass, with zero changes to dispatch or orchestration code.
decorators cause a subclass that forgets to implement a required method to raise
at instantiation time, catching mistakes early.
— the class tree makes the domain model explicit:
communicates intent more clearly than a flat dictionary of handler functions.
— changing a base class method or adding a new attribute risks breaking every subclass. A base class that accumulates shared state becomes a tangled dependency graph.
— subclasses depend on parent internals. If the base class stores state in
and a subclass relies on that, renaming the attribute in the base class silently breaks the subclass.
Deep hierarchies are hard to follow
actually does when there are four levels of overrides requires reading four files. IDE navigation helps, but shallow trees (2–3 levels maximum) are far safer.

— when the goal is mixing in unrelated capabilities (logging, retrying, caching), prefer wrapping objects over inheriting from multiple base classes. Multiple inheritance in Python is possible but introduces MRO complexity and subtle
— Python does not require inheritance to achieve polymorphism. A plain function that accepts any object with
works without a formal hierarchy. Formal ABCs add safety at the cost of ceremony.
When Python resolves a method call it walks the MRO from the leftmost (most specific) class to the rightmost (

). The first class in the MRO that defines the requested method wins. Override a method in a subclass and that override intercepts all calls made through a reference typed to that subclass or any of its descendants.
built-in returns a proxy that continues the MRO walk from the
class, not from the parent. This matters in multiple inheritance:
in a mixin may delegate to another mixin, not directly to the ultimate base class. Cooperative multiple inheritance requires every class in the chain to call
and leaves one or more abstract methods unimplemented cannot be instantiated — Python raises
. This turns a missing-implementation mistake into an early, clear error instead of a confusing
on the runtime type of the object, not on
(or late binding). The method resolved at runtime is the most derived implementation in the MRO. No conditional logic, no type switch — the correct behaviour is selected automatically.
The diagram below shows a three-level hierarchy used in a typical agent framework. The abstract
defines the shared contract. Concrete subclasses implement tool-specific logic. An agent dispatch loop stores a heterogeneous list typed to
without any type checks — polymorphism routes each call to the correct implementation.

content.text: Inheritance is the right choice when two conditions are both true: the relationship is genuinely
(a SearchTool truly is a kind of tool, not just something that happens to have a
method), and the subclass can honour every guarantee the base class makes to its callers (the Liskov Substitution Principle).
when modelling stable domain hierarchies: tools, LLM clients, memory backends, agent loop variants, or output parsers — all follow well-defined contracts and have genuine is-a relationships.
(via inheritance or duck typing) whenever dispatching to one of several interchangeable implementations: routing tool calls, selecting model providers, or choosing memory backends.
for code-reuse only. If the sole reason to subclass is to borrow a helper function, extract that function into a standalone utility instead. The is-a relationship must be real, not convenient.
instance is more flexible than a
) when you want structural typing without requiring callers to inherit from your base class. Useful for accepting objects from third-party libraries.

The implementation pattern for agent extension points follows three steps: define an abstract base class that specifies the contract, implement concrete subclasses, and write the orchestration code against the abstract type only.

in every subclass constructor ensures the parent's initialisation logic runs before the child adds its own state. Forgetting this call is one of the most common sources of hard-to-diagnose bugs in Python class hierarchies.

signature allows each subclass to define its own positional and keyword parameters while remaining callable through the shared interface. The base class signature acts as a minimum contract; subclasses are free to add named parameters on top.
The following runnable example shows how to define an abstract LLM client and two concrete implementations. The agent loop is written once and works with any provider injected at runtime.
This second example demonstrates a full multi-tool registry. Multiple concrete tool classes are registered, then dispatched by name. The dispatcher never inspects types.

Consider a production agent that supports three memory backends: an in-process dictionary (for fast local tests), a Redis store (for single-node deployments), and a distributed vector database (for production semantic search). The orchestration code must remain the same across all environments — only the injected backend changes.
At scale this pattern is critical. Swapping from
in production involves changing exactly one constructor call at the application wiring layer. The hundreds of lines that call
are untouched. This boundary also makes caching and TTL logic easy to test: the in-memory backend replicates TTL behaviour without requiring a Redis server in CI.

The LSP states that any subclass instance must be usable wherever a base class instance is expected, without callers needing to know the difference. A common violation is raising an exception from a method the base class promises will return data.
Mistake 3 — isinstance chains instead of polymorphism
Mistake 4 — deep hierarchies for marginal reuse
looks organised but is fragile. A change in
ripples through four levels. The real reuse is often three lines of code; extract them into a standalone function and keep the hierarchy flat.
Define explicit ABCs for every agent extension point
— tools, LLM clients, memory backends, output parsers, and agent loop variants each deserve an
that documents what subclasses must implement.
— target a maximum of two or three levels. If a fourth level appears, ask whether composition would be cleaner.
does nothing today, a future change adding state will silently break subclasses that skipped it.
in a concrete method body. The ABC mechanism raises at instantiation time, not at the first call, which is far earlier and clearer.
— write a checklist for each abstract method: what type must it return? Which exceptions may it raise? What are valid inputs? Subclasses must honour all.
Prefer Protocol over ABC when accepting third-party objects
defines a structural interface. Objects from external libraries satisfy it without needing to inherit from your ABC.
Document override contracts in the base class docstring
— note which methods subclasses must override, which they may override, and what invariants must hold after every method call.

Python's dynamic dispatch adds a dictionary lookup per method call to resolve the method through the MRO. In practice this overhead is negligible compared to the I/O latency of LLM API calls, network requests, or database queries that agent tools perform. Profiling tool overhead is almost never the bottleneck in a production agent.
The real scalability lever provided by inheritance and polymorphism is

. Injecting a Redis-backed memory store instead of an in-memory one, or a load-balanced LLM client instead of a single-endpoint one, scales the agent horizontally without code changes — just a different object at the composition root.

For hot-path dispatching (thousands of tool calls per second in batch pipelines), consider replacing a dict-of-objects lookup with a pre-built function dispatch table. The polymorphism contract can still be enforced at class-definition time via ABCs while the inner loop uses a plain callable.
) can reduce per-instance memory when tens of thousands of tool objects are alive simultaneously. Define
on the leaf classes; intermediate classes that need to be extended should generally avoid slots because they complicate multiple inheritance.

supports inheritance with full validation. Subclasses inherit field validators from the parent and can add or override fields. LangChain and similar frameworks use Pydantic-backed class hierarchies for tool definitions and runnable interfaces, combining runtime validation with polymorphism.
The table below compares inheritance-based polymorphism against other approaches that achieve similar dispatch flexibility in Python agent codebases.
. A base class that exposes a private helper (
) intended only for its own subclasses may be called by any external subclass — Python has no true access control. Document single-underscore methods clearly and audit subclasses during code review.
method is a privileged execution point. A malicious or buggy subclass could override
to perform unexpected side effects. When tools are loaded from plugins or user-provided code, validate that the tool class genuinely inherits from
before registering it, and run the tool inside a sandboxed process or container when the source is untrusted.
Reliability depends on Liskov compliance. A subclass that
where the base class promises to
breaks any caller that doesn't expect the exception. Define explicit error return types in the abstract interface (e.g.
field) rather than allowing arbitrary exceptions to surface through the dispatcher.
— ensures error handling is centralised in the base class and cannot be accidentally omitted by a subclass author.
. A company has a template describing every employee: they have a name, a department, and a method called
. Individual roles — engineer, designer, manager — fill in the template, specialising
for their role. HR can schedule any employee's day by calling
without knowing their specific role: that is polymorphism. Adding a new role — data scientist — requires writing one new job description; the scheduling system is unchanged.
A complementary mental model: think of the abstract base class as a

. Any device (tool, client, memory backend) that implements the USB-C standard plug connects to any host (the agent loop) without the host caring what device is plugged in. The port contract is fixed; the device side is infinitely extensible.
relationship and shares code; always ensure the child can truly substitute for the parent (Liskov Substitution Principle).
Polymorphism lets a single call site (e.g.,
checks or type switches in the caller.
) enforce contracts at instantiation time, turning missing-implementation bugs into immediate, clear errors.
in child constructors to guarantee the full initialisation chain runs, even if the parent currently does nothing there.
Keep hierarchies shallow (two to three levels maximum); deep override chains are hard to trace and fragile when base classes change.
over forcing external classes to inherit from your ABC.
Centralise error handling in a base-class template method rather than relying on every subclass to catch exceptions consistently.
Composition beats deep inheritance when mixing cross-cutting concerns like retry, logging, and caching — wrap objects rather than stacking mixin classes.
